html:
YFC-Boneagle Electric is not the TWD 70 data-center DAC story in the prior draft. The stock is TWD 30.10 as of May 18, 2026, with a roughly TWD 4.4B market cap, 146.5M shares, 1.4x book, and a trailing P/E near 38x because Q1 2026 was weak. The bullish case is narrower but cleaner: this is a neglected Taiwanese cable, power-cord, and U.S. channel company whose earnings could inflect if U.S. tariff-driven non-China sourcing and higher-spec cable mix finally convert into operating leverage.

The important correction is that YFC’s strategic non-China base is the Philippines, not a proven Vietnam hyperscaler factory. Management says U.S.-bound production has been shifted to the Philippine factory, that non-China global capacity for power cords and network cables is still below 60% of the capacity previously made in China, and that it is also developing ASEAN suppliers in Vietnam and Thailand for branded and OEM/ODM production. That is the real variant perception: YFC is a tiny, leveraged, underfollowed beneficiary of supply-chain fragmentation, not merely a sleepy LAN-cable maker.

The company’s 2024 annual report shows the actual business mix: manufacturing/ODM was 40.9% of revenue, channel distribution 31.1%, and e-commerce 28.0%; by product, power cords were 52.7%, cable 28.1%, consumer electronics 15.2%, and other 4.1%. It sells through owned and affiliated brands, including Monoprice in the U.S., Prime, Bestlink, and Premium-Line, so the business is part factory, part distributor, part consumer/SMB e-commerce channel. That complexity is why screens mislabel it. It is not a pure component supplier, but it has a captive U.S. demand funnel that most Taiwanese micro-cap cable companies do not have.

The best forward-looking angle is not “AI cable hype” in the abstract. It is that the company already makes Cat5e, Cat6, Cat6A, Cat7, Cat8 LAN cabling, fiber patch cords, PoE/LSZH products, industrial signal cables, robotic-arm control cables, solar/energy-storage transmission lines, ADAS high-frequency automotive lines, and Teflon CMP-grade wire for the high-end North American wiring market. Management explicitly names ultra-fine short-length cables for data-center applications as a future development product and says AI, 5G, automation, and robotics are driving investment in high-precision data transmission cables. The market is not paying for that optionality because the income statement still looks like a low-margin distributor with debt.

The recent numbers are messy, which is why the opportunity exists. Revenue fell from TWD 10.10B in 2024 to TWD 8.36B in 2025, but gross margin improved to 28.5%, the highest level in the recent data. Q1 2026 then disappointed: revenue of TWD 2.11B, EPS of -0.32, 28.0% gross margin, and only 1.88% operating margin. The April revenue rebound matters: April 2026 sales were TWD 824.6M, up 34% month over month and 12.1% year over year, bringing Jan-April sales to TWD 2.9B, down only 3.44% year over year after Q1 had been down 8.48%. If May and June hold, Q2 should look materially better than Q1.

The cheapness is forward-cycle, not trailing. At TWD 30.10, the stock is not optically cheap on trailing EPS of about TWD 0.8. But if revenue normalizes back toward TWD 9.5-10.5B and the company turns its 28% gross margin into just 4-5% operating margin, net income can move back toward TWD 280-400M, or EPS of roughly TWD 1.9-2.7. That puts the current price at 11-16x plausible recovery EPS. If higher-spec cables, Philippine utilization, and Monoprice/channel cleanup drive operating margin closer to 6%, EPS can be TWD 3.5-4.0, and the stock is only 7.5-9x that earnings power.

The balance sheet is the main reason not to get reckless. StockAnalysis reports cash of TWD 1.49B against debt of TWD 4.23B, so this is not a net-cash bargain. Goodinfo shows Q1 2026 liabilities at 67.2% of assets, BPS of TWD 21.71, and Q1 operating cash flow was sharply negative. On April 10, 2026, the board approved up to TWD 500M of 3-year, 0% secured convertible bonds to repay bank borrowings. That is rational refinancing, but it also means upside will come with possible dilution. The debt overhang is why the market is right to demand proof.

The stock also has a retail-memory setup. CMoney’s March coverage highlighted the January revenue bounce and the stock’s move toward TWD 36.9-39.7, with momentum framed around improving revenue and fund flows. That trade failed after Q1’s loss, and the stock is back near TWD 30. This is useful because it shows both awareness and disappointment: the name is not undiscovered by local traders, but the first leg was narrative-led. The next leg has to be earnings-led.

The bull case requires three things. First, April’s rebound must become a Q2/Q3 trend, not a one-month catch-up. Second, gross margin must stay around 28% while revenue recovers, proving that high-end/network/industrial mix is real. Third, operating margin needs to break above 4%, because at this company’s current debt level, gross margin without operating conversion is not enough. The upside torque is high precisely because operating profit in 2025 was only 2.56% of sales. A few hundred basis points of margin improvement changes EPS more than the top-line change alone.

What could make this a multi-bagger is not a press release about AI. It would be evidence that Philippine non-China capacity is tight, U.S. customers are shifting more orders, and the company can route that demand through both manufacturing and owned channels. In that world, YFC gets paid twice: as a manufacturer with better utilization and as a channel owner with better sourcing economics. That is the wrong-label opportunity.

The bear case is straightforward. Monoprice has been a chronic drag in the past, tariff changes can raise costs as well as create opportunities, debt remains high, and China-to-Europe competition pressures pricing. If Q2 revenue rolls over, if operating margin stays near 2%, or if the convertible is only a balance-sheet patch rather than a bridge to higher earnings, this is just a levered cable company at 1.4x book.

Bear target: TWD 22, down 27% from TWD 30.10. This assumes Q1 weakness persists, EPS stays below TWD 1.0, the market prices the company near tangible book, and convertible dilution weighs on the stock.

Base target: TWD 43, up 43%. This assumes 2026-2027 revenue recovers toward TWD 9.5-10B, operating margin reaches 4%, EPS approaches TWD 2.5-2.7, and the market pays 16x recovery earnings for a small non-China sourcing beneficiary.

Bull target: TWD 68, up 126%. This assumes revenue returns to TWD 11B+, Philippine/ASEAN sourcing becomes a real bottleneck advantage, data-center/industrial/robotics/high-end cable mix lifts operating margin toward 6%, EPS reaches TWD 3.8-4.2, and the stock rerates to 16-17x earnings.
